%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Frequency Response and Polynomial Evaluation

> **Section Objective:** Learn how continuous-time filter frequency responses are evaluated from transfer-function polynomials and judged against an ideal lowpass target.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Ideal lowpass magnitude response
- Rational transfer function
- Polynomial evaluation at \(s = j\omega\)
- First-order RC lowpass filter
- Cascaded RC filter

## 1. Ideal Lowpass Magnitude Response

This piecewise formula defines the **target** response for a lowpass filter — not a physically realizable circuit. It describes what a perfect filter would do.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(H_{\text{ideal}}(j\omega)\): the ideal filter's frequency response
- \(\omega\): angular frequency in rad/s
- \(\omega_c\): cutoff frequency in rad/s

**When to use it:** When comparing a real lowpass filter against the desired passband (pass everything below \(\omega_c\)) and stopband (block everything above \(\omega_c\)) behavior.

**Exam trigger:** Phrases like "ideal lowpass", "brick-wall response", "unity passband gain", or "perfect stopband attenuation".

**Minimal example:** If \(\omega_c = 1000\) rad/s, then \(|H| = 1\) at \(\omega = 800\) and \(|H| = 0\) at \(\omega = 1500\).

#### Common Misuse
The ideal response is a benchmark, not a buildable filter. Do not treat it as directly implementable.

$$|H_{\text{ideal}}(j\omega)| = \begin{cases} 1, & |\omega| \le \omega_c \\ 0, & |\omega| > \omega_c \end{cases}$$

## 2. Rational Transfer Functions

Many realizable LTIC filters are represented as a ratio of two polynomials in \(s\): a numerator \(B(s)\) divided by a denominator \(A(s)\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(X(s)\): Laplace transform of the input
- \(Y(s)\): Laplace transform of the output
- \(B(s)\): numerator polynomial (feedforward coefficients \(b_k\))
- \(A(s)\): denominator polynomial (feedback coefficients \(a_k\))
- \(M\): degree of the numerator; \(N\): degree of the denominator

**When to use it:** When the system is described by polynomial coefficients, roots, poles, or zeros.

**Exam trigger:** "Rational transfer function", "feedforward coefficients", "feedback coefficients", or "evaluate the response from numerator and denominator".

**Minimal example:** Setting \(B(s) = 1\) and \(A(s) = RCs + 1\) gives a one-pole lowpass structure.

#### Common Misuse
Reversing the numerator and denominator coefficient vectors — always confirm which polynomial is on top.

$$H(s)=\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}=\frac{B(s)}{A(s)}=\frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_{k+N-M}s^{M-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k s^{N-k}}$$

## 3. Frequency response by substituting s = jω

The frequency response is obtained by evaluating the transfer function on the **imaginary axis**: replace every \(s\) in \(H(s)\) with \(j\omega\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(j\): imaginary unit, \(j^2 = -1\)
- \(\omega\): angular frequency in rad/s
- \(B(j\omega)\): numerator polynomial evaluated at \(s = j\omega\)
- \(A(j\omega)\): denominator polynomial evaluated at \(s = j\omega\)

**When to use it:** Whenever a continuous-time transfer function must be plotted or evaluated versus frequency.

**Exam trigger:** "Compute frequency response", "magnitude response", or "evaluate at frequency vector omega".

**MATLAB idea:** `polyval(B,j*omega)./polyval(A,j*omega)` evaluates the numerator and denominator at all frequencies in a single vectorized call.

#### Common Misuse
Using frequency in Hz directly when the formula expects rad/s — always convert: \(\omega = 2\pi f\).

$$H(j\omega)=\frac{B(j\omega)}{A(j\omega)}$$

## 4. Polynomial Evaluation from Coefficient Vectors

MATLAB's `polyval` evaluates a polynomial from its coefficient vector using **descending powers** of the argument.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(A = [a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_N]\): coefficient vector, first entry for the highest power
- \(a_k\): coefficient multiplying \((j\omega)^{N-k}\)
- \(N\): degree of the polynomial
- \(j\omega\): the evaluation point on the imaginary axis

**When to use it:** When coefficients are already known and a frequency vector must be evaluated efficiently.

**Exam trigger:** Code such as `polyval(A,j*omega)` or a request to compute \(H(j\omega)\) from coefficient vectors.

**Minimal example:** If \(A = [RC, 1]\), then `polyval(A,j*omega)` computes \(RC(j\omega) + 1\), which is the denominator of the RC lowpass filter.

#### Common Misuse
Entering coefficients in **ascending** instead of descending powers — e.g., writing \([1, RC]\) when \([RC, 1]\) is required.

$$\operatorname{polyval}(A,j\omega)=\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k(j\omega)^{N-k}$$

## 5. First-Order RC Lowpass Filter

The RC circuit has transfer function \(H_{RC}(s) = (RCs + 1)^{-1}\). Substituting \(s = j\omega\) and taking the magnitude gives the formula above.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(R\): resistance in ohms
- \(C\): capacitance in farads
- \(RC\): time constant in seconds
- \(\omega\): angular frequency in rad/s

**When to use it:** For a one-pole RC lowpass magnitude calculation or cutoff frequency design.

**Exam trigger:** "RC lowpass", "one-pole filter", "half-power point", or \(|H(j\omega_c)| = 1/\sqrt{2}\).

At the cutoff frequency \(\omega_c = 1/(RC)\), the magnitude equals \(1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707\) — the half-power point.

**Minimal example:** For a 3 kHz cutoff, use \(\omega_c = 2\pi(3000)\) rad/s, not 3000.

#### Common Misuse
Forgetting the square root in the denominator, or substituting frequency in Hz directly instead of converting to rad/s first.

$$|H_{RC}(j\omega)|=|(j\omega RC+1)^{-1}|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(RC\omega)^2}}$$

## 6. Cascaded RC Filters

Cascading \(N\) buffered (isolated) RC stages raises the single-stage response to the \(N\)th power, producing an \(N\)th-order lowpass filter with a repeated pole at \(s = -1/(RC)\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(N\): number of identical buffered stages
- \(R\), \(C\): shared component values per stage
- \(H_{RC}(s)\): single-stage transfer function

**When to use it:** When several identical buffered stages are connected to improve stopband attenuation beyond what a single stage can achieve.

**Exam trigger:** "Cascade", "N stages", "Nth-order filter", "repeated pole", or "use `poly` to expand roots".

**Design pattern:** For \(N = 10\) stages, the cutoff is designed using:

$$R = \frac{\sqrt{2^{1/10}-1}}{C\,\omega_c}$$

This corrects for the fact that cascading shifts the \(-3\) dB point.

#### Common Misuse
Assuming cascades only multiply gains without changing rolloff sharpness — in reality, each added stage makes the stopband attenuation significantly steeper.

$$H_{\text{cascade}}(s)=[H_{RC}(s)]^N=(RCs+1)^{-N}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Ideal lowpass: \(|H_{\text{ideal}}(j\omega)| = 1\) for \(|\omega| \le \omega_c\), and \(0\) beyond — a target, not a buildable filter.
- Frequency response: substitute \(s = j\omega\) to get \(H(j\omega) = B(j\omega)/A(j\omega)\) from any rational transfer function.
- Polynomial evaluation: \(\operatorname{polyval}(A, j\omega) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k (j\omega)^{N-k}\) — coefficients must be in descending power order.
- RC lowpass magnitude: \(|H_{RC}(j\omega)| = 1/\sqrt{1 + (RC\omega)^2}\); cutoff at \(\omega_c = 1/(RC)\) where magnitude equals \(1/\sqrt{2}\).
- Cascade of \(N\) stages: \(H_{\text{cascade}}(s) = (RCs+1)^{-N}\) — higher \(N\) gives sharper stopband rolloff.

*Next, we use higher-order filter design tools to improve the approximation even further.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
